interactions.summary: He announces a shift from literature to AI and introduces Karen Hao as the organizing text for the rest of the semester.
Jiang has a classmate (likely student) read a passage opening the text, and uses that reading as the first textual anchor for the class.
He invites corrections and asks students to interrupt with questions and criticism while class proceeds.

speaker_notes.note: The short 'So stop' prompt is classroom reading coordination, not a substantive claim source.

claims.claim: Jiang plans a semester-long AI sequence using Karen Hao's journalism as core reading, with his own skeptical interpretation overriding his earlier neutral stance toward AI safety projects.
He reports that OpenAI's stated altruistic mission now appears to him as a mechanism for centralizing power and resources at imperial scale.

signature_moments.moment: OpenAI's mission recast from idealism to empire-building
signature_moments.source_phrase: sacred, sincere stroke of idealism ... uniquely potent formula for consolidating resources
signature_moments.why_it_matters: Establishes the episode's central interpretive tension between initial idealist narrative and later control thesis.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: The claim that AI institutions evolve from utility projects into concentrated-capability structures with imperial aims.
A classroom mechanism where students read primary-source paragraphs so the instructor can interrogate the implications.

chronology_notes.note: The source moves from David Bromwich editorial framing into a new curriculum arc titled around AI theory, with explicit reading assignments and class interaction rituals.

uncertainty_notes.note: The passage has multiple disfluencies in the transcript around 'ask your power structure'; preserve gist and avoid verbatim precision for that clause.
